title: Jesus who restores part 2
scripture_text: ‘’Afterwards, Jesus appeared again to his disciples by the Sea of Galilee. It happened this way: Simon Peter, Thomas (also known as Didymus), Nathanael from Cana in Galilee, the sons of Zebedee, and two other disciples were together “I’m going out to fish,” Simon Peter told them, and they said, “We’ll go with you.” So they went out and got into the boat, but that night they caught nothing. Early in the morning, Jesus stood on the shore, but the disciples did not realise that it was Jesus. He called out to them “Friends, haven’t you any fish?” “No,” they answered. He said, “Throw your net on the right side of the boat and you will find some.” When they did, they were unable to haul the net in because of the large number of fish. Then the disciple whom Jesus loved, said to Peter, “It is the Lord!” As soon as Simon Peter heard him say, “It is the Lord,” he wrapped his outer garment around him (for he had taken it off) and jumped into the water. The other disciples followed in the boat, towing the net full of fish, for they were not far from shore, about a hundred yards. When they landed, they saw a fire of burning coals there with fish on it, and some bread. Jesus said to them, “Bring some of the fish you have just caught.” So Simon Peter climbed back into the boat and dragged the net ashore. It was full of large fish, 153, but even with so many, the net was not torn. Jesus said to them, “Come and have breakfast.” None of the disciples dared ask him, “Who are you?” They knew it was the Lord. Jesus came, took the bread and gave it to them, and did the same with the fish. This was now the third time Jesus appeared to his disciples after he was raised from the dead.’’
devotional:
Jesus Reinstates Peter

‘’When they had finished eating, Jesus said to Simon Peter. “Simon son of John, do you love me more than these?” “Yes, Lord,” he said, “you know that I love you.” Jesus said, “Feed my lambs.” Again Jesus said, “Simon son of John, do you love me?” He answered, “Yes, Lord, you know that I love you.”Jesus said, “Take care of my sheep.” The third time he said to him, “Simon son of John, do you love me?” Peter was hurt because Jesus asked him the third time, “Do you love me?” He said, “Lord, you know all things, you know that I love you.”Jesus said, “Feed my sheep. Very truly I tell you, when you were younger you dressed yourself and went where you wanted; but when you are old you will stretch out your hands, and someone else will dress you and lead you where you do not want to go.” Jesus said this to indicate the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God. Then he said to him, “Follow me!” Peter turned and saw that the disciple whom Jesus loved was following them. (This was the one who had leaned back against Jesus at the supper and had said, “Lord, who is going to betray you?”) When Peter saw him, he asked, “Lord, what about him?” Jesus answered, “If I want him to remain alive until I return, what is that to you? You must follow me.” Because of this, the rumour spread among the believers that this disciple would not die. But Jesus did not say that he would not die; he only said, “If I want him to remain alive until I return, what is that to you?” This is the disciple who testifies to these things and who wrote them down. We know that his testimony is true. Jesus did many other things as well. If every one of them were written down, I suppose that even the whole world would not have room for the books that would be written.’’

To fish or not to fish? That is the question

When Peter and the other disciples went fishing, I wonder, what was going on in their hearts and minds?

There has been a lot of debate about whether Peter was being obedient or

disobedient when he went fishing. There are a lot of arguments that can be

made either way. However, I think that the question about Peter’s obedience in

this context is the wrong question to ask. I am not saying that obedience is not

an important issue. However, there are times when we can over simplify things

and just have two categories where everything falls into, obedience or disobedience. As we shall see, Peter’s issues were much deeper and more profound than obedience or disobedience.

1. Perhaps Peter was motivated by a sense of shame.

The bible does not tell us what Peter is thinking or feeling, but we can be certain that he was in a very vulnerable condition. He was in danger of being redefined by his failure. He had betrayed Jesus three times with oaths and curses. He had boasted that although everyone else would forsake Jesus he would not. How could he ever get back from this? He is now motivated by shame, he has lost all his confidence, so he retreats to where he feels safe and in control, to a place he knows well, a comfort zone.

Perhaps Peter went fishing for answers.

What have I done? What were those three years about? Who was Jesus? Where is he? Who am I? What will I do now? Where will I go? What will happen to me? Peter is searching for meaning, a way forward, a place in life. Maybe the reason he went fishing was to ease his pain, trying to get beyond his guilt, possibly believing that all his hope and desires to follow Jesus were gone. He found it easier to go fishing than deal with the consequences and the emotional turmoil of his failure.

We all know what it is to fail, we all know what it feels like when we want to disappear, to become unseen. When we are struggling with personal failure, there is something comforting about being in the place of isolation where we are unnoticed. Our shame and guilt create in us a strong desire to get away from it all. We are often driven by intense sense of shame, and we wrongly conclude that we are unworthy of acceptance and love. It is in this lonely place where we need to encounter the risen Lord, and yet, if we are honest, this is the last place we want to encounter him. I love the fact that Jesus came to Peter where he was. Peter was not in prayer meeting, or at a special retreat. No he was in the mess and confusion of his everyday life. Jesus met him in the last place you would expect Jesus to be.

Our everyday lives need to be touched and influenced by Jesus and his resurrection grace. We need to be open to the process of restoration, so we will not be defined by our failure.

Peter experienced the mercy of an empty net.

We read in verse 5 ‘He called out to them, “Friends, haven’t you any fish?” “No,”They answered.’ A better reading of this verse would be, ‘He called out, you haven’t caught any fish have you?’ This is more of a statement than a question. It is as if it had been planned or ordained that Simon Peter and the other disciples would catch no fish. In the frustration and disappointment of an empty net, Peter learned that he could not fix what was going on in his heart, and fish in a net was not going to change anything. Sometimes we think, ‘if only,….’ believing that if the ‘if only,’ blank was filled in, we would feel better about ourselves. We often try to medicate our pain with stuff when what we really need is renewal in our relationship with Jesus.

Peter needed to learn that his eagerness to please Jesus would not remove the need to have a difficult conversation.

As you read this passage you get the impression that Peter wanted to please Jesus in an attempt to replace the pain he had caused. Listen to these words. ‘Then the disciple whom Jesus loved said to Peter, “It is the Lord!” As soon as Simon Peter heard him say, “It is the Lord,” he wrapped his outer garment around him (for he had taken it off) and jumped into the water….. So Simon Peter climbed back into the boat and dragged the net ashore. It was full of large fish.’

Look at what Peter did.

As soon as he heard, he wrapped his outer garment around himself, he jumped into the water, He climbed back into the boat He dragged the net ashore. He made every effort to please the Lord. I am sure the Lord appreciated his eagerness, however, eagerness and the desire to please, would not go far enough to deal with the real pain of his heart.

When we fail and feel compromised, it is very easy to think ‘if I try harder, or if I do better then I won’t fall into the same trap, or make the same mistakes.’

Enthusiasm is important but it will not address the reasons why we failed in the first place. Peter’s enthusiasm was an honest expression of his desire to please Jesus, but there was a need to go beyond enthusiasm to healing and wholeness. Sometimes that can be a process.

Peter needed to learn that acceptance proceeds restoration.

Look at what verse 10 says. ‘’And then Jesus says to them, ‘Bring some of the

fish you have just caught.’ Jesus is asking His disciples for something. Can you

imagine what that must have communicated to these disciples? Perhaps the

disciples think that they have nothing Jesus needs; they have nothing to offer

Jesus whatsoever, he doesn’t need them. But Jesus says, ‘No, I want to eat

your fish. I value what you have caught. I value what you have to bring to me.’

The second thing, Jesus goes to show how he accepted Peter and the rest of the

disciples was he said ‘Come and have breakfast.’

Being invited to a meal at the time of Jesus signified deep friendship, intimacy and unity. So before Jesus chose to have a difficult conversation with Peter, Jesus did everything he could to help Peter feel accepted.

This is very interesting because we usually work the other way round. We think

in terms of ‘Sort yourself out then we will accept you,’ but we need to understand that people change because they are loved. It is only in the context of accepting love that significant life-changing confrontation and conversation can take place.

Jesus needed to have a difficult conversation with Peter.‘’When they had finished eating, Jesus said to Simon Peter, “Simon son of John, do you love me more than these?” “Yes, Lord,” he said, “you know that I love you.” Jesus said, “Feed my lambs.”1 Again Jesus said, “Simon son of John, do you love me?” He answered, “Yes, Lord, you know that I love you.” Jesus said, “Take care of my sheep. The third time he said to him, “Simon son of John, do you love me?” Peter was hurt because Jesus asked him the third time, “Do you love me?” He said, “Lord, you know all things; you know that I love you.” Jesus said, “Feed my sheep.”

This was a very difficult conversation for Peter, we are told that he was hurt. The word John uses carries the idea of heaviness or distress. Sometimes Jesus needs to come to us and ask us uncomfortable questions, questions that are critically important and life defining.

Consider what Christ doesn’t do. He doesn’t try to make Peter feel guilty. He doesn’t humiliate him publicly. He doesn’t ask him, “Are you sorry for what you did?” He doesn’t make him promise to do better. He just asks one question.
prayer: Father thank you that you want to restore us, and thank you that you’re patient with us and you don’t humiliate us in the process.
html:
Jesus who restores part 2

Study Text: John 21
‘’Afterwards, Jesus appeared again to his disciples by the Sea of Galilee. It happened this way: Simon Peter, Thomas (also known as Didymus), Nathanael from Cana in Galilee, the sons of Zebedee, and two other disciples were together “I’m going out to fish,” Simon Peter told them, and they said, “We’ll go with you.” So they went out and got into the boat, but that night they caught nothing. Early in the morning, Jesus stood on the shore, but the disciples did not realise that it was Jesus. He called out to them “Friends, haven’t you any fish?” “No,” they answered. He said, “Throw your net on the right side of the boat and you will find some.” When they did, they were unable to haul the net in because of the large number of fish. Then the disciple whom Jesus loved, said to Peter, “It is the Lord!” As soon as Simon Peter heard him say, “It is the Lord,” he wrapped his outer garment around him (for he had taken it off) and jumped into the water. The other disciples followed in the boat, towing the net full of fish, for they were not far from shore, about a hundred yards. When they landed, they saw a fire of burning coals there with fish on it, and some bread. Jesus said to them, “Bring some of the fish you have just caught.” So Simon Peter climbed back into the boat and dragged the net ashore. It was full of large fish, 153, but even with so many, the net was not torn. Jesus said to them, “Come and have breakfast.” None of the disciples dared ask him, “Who are you?” They knew it was the Lord. Jesus came, took the bread and gave it to them, and did the same with the fish. This was now the third time Jesus appeared to his disciples after he was raised from the dead.’’

We know that his testimony is true. Jesus did many other things as well. If every one of them were written down, I suppose that even the whole world would not have room for the books that would be written.’’

The bible does not tell us what Peter is thinking or feeling, but we can be certain that he was in a very vulnerable condition. He was in danger of being redefined by his failure. He is now motivated by shame, he has lost all his confidence, so he retreats to where he feels safe and in control, to a place he knows well, a comfort zone.

Our everyday lives need to be touched and influenced by Jesus and his resurrection grace. We need to be open to the process of restoration, so we will not be defined by our failure.

We read in verse 5 ‘He called out to them, “Friends, haven’t you any fish?” “No,”They answered.’ A better reading of this verse would be, ‘He called out, you haven’t caught any fish have you?’ This is more of a statement than a question. Bible in a year reading plan: - Job 36-38
